values.content:
=You are a professional hardware security report writer.

Below is a raw automated fault-injection and mitigation audit report. Rewrite it as a polished executive-level security advisory.

Use exactly these sections:
1. Executive Summary
2. Module Under Test (name, type, critical register identified)
3. Vulnerability Details (each bit attacked, state value before/after, jump distance, severity)
4. Mitigation Strategy Applied (what hardening was applied and why)
5. Validation Results (did re-attack confirm mitigation)
6. Recommendations

RAW REPORT:
{{ $json.disclosure_report }}

parameters.jsCode:
const attempt = $runIndex + 1;

const aggItem = $('Aggregate Logs').first().json;
const valItem = $('Validation Log Parser').first().json;

const enrichedReport = [
  "=== ORIGINAL FAULT INJECTION FINDINGS ===",
  aggItem.vuln_report || "(none)",
  "",
  `=== PREVIOUS HARDENING ATTEMPT ${attempt} FAILED ===`,
  `  Bit Re-Attacked : ${valItem.bit_targeted}`,
  `  PC Before       : ${valItem.pc_before}`,
  `  PC After        : ${valItem.pc_after}`,
  `  Jump Distance   : ${valItem.jump_distance_bytes} bytes`,
  `  Result          : ${valItem.result_summary}`,
  "",
  "Try a stronger strategy:",
  "  Attempt 1 failed -> Add jump-distance watchdog on top of TMR",
  "  Attempt 2 failed -> Add PC alignment enforcement (pc[1:0] must be 2'b00)",
  "  Attempt 3 failed -> Add full ECC with CPU reset on 2+ flips detected",
  "  Attempt 4+ failed -> Combine ALL strategies above"
].join("\n");

return [{
  json: {
    has_serious:     true,
    vuln_report:     enrichedReport,
    total_trials:    aggItem.total_trials    || 0,
    serious_count:   aggItem.serious_count   || 0,
    worst_bit:       valItem.bit_targeted,
    worst_jump:      valItem.jump_distance_bytes,
    worst_pc_before: valItem.pc_before,
    worst_pc_after:  valItem.pc_after,
    retry_attempt:   attempt,
    still_vulnerable: true   // we only get here if still vulnerable
  }
}];

values.content:
=You are a Verilog static analysis expert. Analyse the Verilog source code below and return ONLY a single JSON object — no markdown, no backticks, no explanation.

ANALYSE THIS CODE:
{{ $json.decoded }}

EXTRACT and return this exact JSON structure:
{
  "module_name": "<the module name from 'module X (...)'  >",
  "primary_state_reg": "<the most critical sequential state register — typically named pc, ip, PC, state, addr, counter, or similar. This is the register whose bit-flip will cause the most critical failure>",
  "primary_reg_width": <integer bit width of primary_state_reg, e.g. 32>,
  "clock_port": "<name of the clock input port, e.g. clk, clock, CLK>",
  "reset_port": "<name of the reset input port, e.g. reset, rst, rst_n, RESET — or null if none>",
  "reset_active_low": <true if reset is active-low (rst_n style), false if active-high>,
  "all_ports": "<complete port list string exactly as it appears in the module declaration, e.g. clk, reset, instr, result>",
  "port_declarations": "<the full port declaration lines, e.g. input clk; input reset; input [31:0] instr; output [31:0] result;">",
  "other_state_regs": ["<list of other important sequential registers worth fault-testing, e.g. sp, lr, flags>"],
  "module_type": "<one of: cpu, fsm, counter, memory_controller, uart, alu, arbiter, other>",
  "has_program_counter": <true if the module has a program-counter-like register, false otherwise>
}

RULES:
- Return ONLY the raw JSON object. No markdown fences. No explanation.
- If a field cannot be determined, use null for strings and false for booleans.
- For primary_state_reg: prefer registers updated on clock edge that affect control flow.

=You are a hardware security expert specialising in fault-injection testing for ANY Verilog design.

You will be given the full Verilog source of a module. Your job is to:

Identify the module name, clock port, reset port, and most critical state register automatically from the source.

Generate a complete self-contained fault-injection testbench that works with THIS specific module.

VERILOG SOURCE TO ANALYSE:
{{ $json.prompt }}

BIT HISTORY (already tested — do NOT choose these again): {{ $json.history }}

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════
GENERATION RULES — read the source above and follow these:
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════

RULE 1 — MODULE NAME (CRITICAL — violations cause compile failure):
Read the module declaration line (module <NAME> ...) from the VERILOG SOURCE above.
The ONLY valid module name is the one declared in that source.
Use THAT exact name to instantiate: <NAME> uut (...);
NEVER invent, guess, or substitute a different module name.
VERIFY: before writing the instantiation, re-read the source and confirm the name matches.

RULE 2 — PORT CONNECTION (CRITICAL — wrong ports cause elaboration failure):
Read EVERY port from the module declaration in the source above.
ONLY use port names that literally appear in the module (...) declaration — no others.
For INPUT ports: declare a reg with the SAME name and connect: .portname(portname).
For OUTPUT ports: leave UNCONNECTED with empty parens: .portname().
Do NOT invent, rename, or omit any port.
Do NOT declare a wire for output ports — access outputs via uut.<portname> directly.
VERIFY each port name against the source before writing the instantiation.

RULE 3 — PRIMARY STATE REGISTER:
Identify the most critical sequential register in the design (PC, state, or count).
This is the register you will inject the fault into.
Call it PRIMARY_REG in your testbench (replace with actual signal path uut.<reg_name>).

RULE 4 — CLOCK:
Read the clock port name from the source. Generate a clock at 100MHz (period=10ns).

RULE 5 — RESET:
Read the reset port name and polarity from the source.
Active-high: reset=1; #20; reset=0; | Active-low: reset=0; #20; reset=1;

RULE 6 — DRIVE INPUTS:
After reset, drive non-clock/reset inputs with non-zero values. Change values every 20ns.

RULE 7 — LET IT RUN:
Wait at least 200ns after reset before injecting the fault.

RULE 8 — FAULT INJECTION (exact sequence):
state_before_fault = uut.<primary_reg>;
fault_buffer = uut.<primary_reg> ^ ('h1 << [BIT_NUMBER]);
force uut.<primary_reg> = fault_buffer;
#1;
release uut.<primary_reg>;

RULE 9 — MANDATORY DISPLAY (exact format):
$display("Old PC = %h, New PC = %h", state_before_fault, fault_buffer);
$display("Bit %0d flipped", [BIT_NUMBER]);

RULE 10 — WAIT AND FINISH:
After injection wait 200ns, then $finish.

RULE 11 — MONITOR BLOCK:
initial begin $display("START_LOG"); $monitor("Time=%0t state=%h", $time, uut.<primary_reg>); end

RULE 12 — NO INLINE INITIALIZATION (CRITICAL):
All 'reg' variables (including fault_buffer and state_before_fault) must be declared at the module level, BEFORE the initial blocks.
Do NOT use 'reg [WIDTH-1:0] x = value;' or declare 'reg' inside an 'initial' block.
Declare them first, then assign values inside 'initial' blocks.

BIT SELECTION:
Choose one bit from 0 to (WIDTH-1) NOT in history. Write the integer directly.

OUTPUT:
Return ONLY raw Verilog code. Start with: module test; End with: endmodule. No markdown. No backticks. No comments.
